captions.text: In this lesson, you'll build the core infrastructure
for memory management in an AI agent.
You'll implement a MemoryManager class that abstracts away
the complexity of reading and writing to different memory types,
and you'll connect this to an Oracle database
for persistent storage and retrieval.
Let's get started.
In this lesson, we'll look at the memory manager.
We'll also discuss its key components.
And we'll dive into the code and build this memory manager.
Let's start by recapping what the memory manager is.
The Memory Manager is a software harness
that abstracts away the complexity of accessing memory.
It provides a unified interface to read and write
to different types of memory stores,
which in this case will be tables within an Oracle database.
This harness, this piece of code,
is what enables the agent to access memory,
in a structured and reliable manner.
Let's talk about the key components of the Memory Manager.
The first one is the Memory Store.
Now a Memory Store is a computational representation
of a specific type of memory.
So for example, conversational memory,
which we defined in a previous lesson,
is a type of memory and a computational representation of it,
which in this case is a table within the Oracle database.
Now, we can have multiple types of memory stores.
So you can have the conversational memory store,
entity memory store, workflow memory store,
knowledge base memory store, and also summary memory store.
All of these are what we refer to as Memory Stores.
Now, another key component of the Memory Manager is the Store Manager.
Now the Store Manager can be seen as
a high-level orchestrator of memory stores.
Its primary function is to create and manage
the lifecycle of these different memory stores.
So the Store Manager is in charge of creating,
updating, deleting, and reading
the different memory stores.
Now that we understand the key components,
let's dive into the code and start building.
The first thing we're going to do is connect to our Oracle database.
Now, for this, we need to install the Oracle DB Python client.
We need to import the necessary modules,
create a connection with our credentials,
and then pass in our Oracle database connection string.
Now once the connection is established,
we're going to verify the connection is working,
by running a simple query on the database.
Now once that's confirmed,
we're going to create the memory store tables.
Now the first memory store we'll create is the conversational memory store.
Now this will be a SQL-based table.
Now typically,
conversational memory is stored in a SQL-based table
because we want to maintain the sequence of the conversations,
and we also want to have time-ordered retrieval.
So we'll create a SQL statement,
defining our table schema.
Now we want the ID of the message to be primary key,
we want to have a thread ID,
so we can have multiple conversation threads,
we want to have a role, which is the user or assistant,
we want to have the message content,
and also a timestamp for when the message was created.
We also want to add some metadata,
for example, the message being summarized, flagged, or marked as read.
So that we can do some post-processing on the messages.
So we have a create_table_if_not_exists method,
that ensures that we don't recreate tables that already exist.
Now after we execute this, the table will be created.
Now for the knowledge base,
and the entity memory and workflow,
we're going to be using a vector-based store,
because for these types of memory,
we want to perform similarity search,
which is the main advantage of using vector stores.
Now for vector stores,
the Oracle database provides a vector data type,
so we're going to use the Oracle AI vector search.
Now to create a vector store, we need to specify the vector data type,
and also the vector dimension.
Now for this demo, we're going to use an embedding model
which is the paraphrase-mpnet-base-v2.
This model has 768 dimensions.
So we'll create a knowledge base memory store,
which will have the ID, the content, the embedding,
and also some metadata.
Now for entity memory,
this will capture entity information such as people, places,
and organizations. This is an important type of memory
because it allows us to identify
who and what an entity is within a conversation.
Now we'll create a table for entity memory.
Now for workflow memory,
this is where we capture the steps and outcomes
of the agent in accomplishing a specific task.
Now this is a very important type of memory
because it allows the agent to reference
the steps it has taken in the past to accomplish objectives.
We'll also create a summary memory store.
Now summary memory is where we store summaries
of conversations.
Now this will be important for context window management,
when we want to compress a long conversation,
we'll place the summary in this store.
Now there's also another type of memory store called the tool log,
and this is where we store the logs of tool execution.
Now this is important because it allows us
to trace the steps that an agent has taken in a workflow.
Now let's create the Store Manager class.
The Store Manager will orchestrate all the memory stores.
Now the Store Manager has a create memory store method,
which will check the type of memory store to be created.
If it's a SQL-based store,
it will create a SQL-based store,
and if it's a vector-based store,
it will create a vector store.
Now the Store Manager will also have a get memory store method,
that returns the memory store for a given type.
And it also has a method to create all the stores at once.
Now let's create the Memory Manager class.
Now the Memory Manager is the primary interface
for the agent to access memory.
It abstracts away the complexity of reading and writing
to different memory stores.
Now it has several read and write methods for each type of memory.
So for conversational memory,
it has a write_conversational_memory method,
and a read_conversational_memory method.
For entity memory,
it has write_entity_memory and read_entity_memory.
For knowledge base memory,
it has write_knowledge_base and read_knowledge_base.
For workflow memory,
it has write_workflow_memory and read_workflow_memory.
And for summary memory,
it has write_summary_memory and read_summary_memory.
Now we can test the Memory Manager.
First, we'll instantiate the Memory Manager,
passing in the memory store.
Now let's write to the conversational memory.
We'll write a simple message.
Now let's read the conversational memory.
Now let's write to the entity memory.
Now let's read the entity memory.
Now let's write to the knowledge base memory.
Now let's read the knowledge base memory.
Now let's write to the workflow memory.
Now let's read the workflow memory.
Now we've successfully built the Memory Manager.
This is the core infrastructure for memory management in an AI agent.
In future lessons, we'll build on this foundation
to add more advanced memory operations.
Well done.
